title: Why Cross-Encoders Can't Index Ahead of Time
concept: Cross-encoder scores require the query at inference time, making them too slow for searching millions of documents directly.

exercises.prompt: How does a Cross-Encoder compare a query and a document to achieve high accuracy?

options.text: By embedding the query and document separately and doing a dot product.
By feeding the query and document together into the model to allow direct attention.
By ignoring the query and only analyzing the document's keywords.

exercises.explain: A Cross-Encoder feeds the query and document together as a single input, allowing attention mechanisms to compare them word-for-word.

cards.title: The Speed Bottleneck
cards.body: We know Cross-Encoders are incredibly accurate because they compare queries and documents word-for-word. But there is a massive catch: this superpower makes them incredibly slow for searching large databases. Let's find out why.

cards.title: No Pre-Computation allowed
cards.body: Unlike Bi-Encoders, which let us pre-calculate and store vector embeddings for documents, a Cross-Encoder must process the query and document together. This means we cannot index our documents ahead of time, because the model needs the query to output any score.

cards.title: The Live Translator Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a translator who must read a unique letter and a specific response together to score how well they match. They cannot prepare any scores beforehand because they don't know what the letter will say until it arrives.

cards.title: Real-time Evaluation
cards.body: If a user searches for 'How to bake bread?', a Cross-Encoder cannot just look up a pre-baked vector for a recipe page. It must actively feed 'How to bake bread?' alongside each recipe page through the entire transformer network to get a relevance score.

cards.title: The Vector Database Trap
cards.body: You might think we can just save time by pre-running the Cross-Encoder on our documents. But because the model relies on the interaction between the query and document words, there are no individual document vectors to save ahead of time!

cards.title: Pre-indexing vs. Run-time Computation
cards.body: See how Bi-encoders let us prepare beforehand, while Cross-encoders force us to do all the heavy lifting at the very last second.

left.text: Pre-computes and saves document vectors ahead of time. Fast lookup.

right.text: Must process query + document together at run-time. No pre-computing.

exercises.prompt: Why is it impossible to index documents ahead of time using a Cross-Encoder?

options.text: They are not compatible with modern GPU acceleration.
They require the query at run-time to perform any similarity calculations.
They compress documents too much, losing vital details.
They only work with short, one-word documents.

exercises.explain: Cross-encoders cannot pre-calculate and store document embeddings because they require both the query and the document together as a single input to produce a score.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Cross-encoders are the best tool for directly searching through millions of documents in less than a millisecond.
exercises.explain: Because Cross-Encoders cannot pre-compute document vectors, running them against millions of documents at query time is too slow for real-time applications.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about when Cross-Encoder calculations must occur.
exercises.sentence: Because we cannot pre-calculate scores, a Cross-Encoder must perform all heavy model computations at inference time.
exercises.explain: Cross-encoders need both inputs simultaneously, meaning all calculations must happen at inference time when the user runs their query.

exercises.prompt: Identify which statement accurately explains the indexing limitation of Cross-Encoders.

options.text: Cross-encoders pre-calculate embeddings for all documents and store them in a database, allowing sub-millisecond search over billions of items.
Because a Cross-Encoder needs both the query and document simultaneously to calculate a score, it cannot pre-index documents and is too slow to evaluate millions of documents at query time.
Cross-encoders do not use neural networks, which makes them fast to index but highly inaccurate compared to Bi-Encoders.

exercises.explain: The AI text correctly identifies the fundamental limitation of Cross-Encoders: they must compare the query and document together at query time, making them too slow to search millions of documents directly.

exercises.prompt: If you want to search a database of 10 million documents using only a Cross-Encoder, what slow operation must you perform for every single search?

options.text: Store the document embeddings in a vector database before the query arrives
Calculate the query embedding once and compare it using simple dot product math
Run the Cross-Encoder model on the query paired with every single document at search time
Pre-compute all possible query-document pairs in an offline database

exercises.explain: Because Cross-Encoders require both the query and the document to be fed into the network together, you cannot pre-calculate document embeddings or index them in a vector database. Computing scores for millions of documents at query-time is computationally impossible for real-time search.
